name: coding-standards
description: React 19 and TypeScript coding standards for Portfolio Buddy 2. Use when: writing new components, reviewing code, refactoring, or ensuring consistency. Contains component patterns, TypeScript rules, and best practices.
capabilities: Defines React 19 component structure patterns with hooks
Specifies TypeScript standards including no `any` types
author: 5MinFutures
securitySummary: This skill contains only markdown documentation with TypeScript code examples. All 91 static findings are false positives: hash strings triggered C2/crypto keywords, mathematical formulas were misidentified as cryptographic code, and TypeScript patterns were flagged as system reconnaissance. No executable code, network calls, file system access, or environment variable usage exists.
valueStatement: Portfolio Buddy 2 developers need consistent coding patterns to maintain code quality. This skill provides documented standards for React 19 components, TypeScript types, and project organization.
limitations: Reference documentation only, does not enforce standards
Specific to Portfolio Buddy 2 project structure
No automated linting or code checking

useCases.title: New component creation
useCases.description: Follow hook patterns and TypeScript standards when building new React components for the portfolio application.

useCases.title: Review code contributions
useCases.description: Verify pull requests meet documented standards for component size, types, and import organization.

useCases.title: Technical debt reduction
useCases.description: Use documented patterns to refactor oversized components like PortfolioSection.tsx into smaller pieces.

promptTemplates.title: Component pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a new React 19 component following the coding-standards skill. Use functional component with TypeScript, hooks at top level, useMemo for derived state, and useCallback for handlers.

promptTemplates.title: TypeScript types
promptTemplates.prompt: Define TypeScript interfaces for my data using the coding-standards skill. No any types, use strict null checks, and add JSDoc comments for complex functions.

promptTemplates.title: Refactor component
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me refactor this oversized component using patterns from coding-standards. Extract sub-components and move logic to custom hooks to stay under 200 lines.

promptTemplates.title: Import organization
promptTemplates.prompt: Organize my imports following the coding-standards skill: React libraries first, then internal hooks, then utils, then types.

bestPractices: Always define TypeScript interfaces for data structures instead of using `any`
Keep components under 200 lines by extracting sub-components and custom hooks
Use React hooks at top level only with complete dependency arrays
antiPatterns: Using `any` type instead of proper TypeScript interfaces
Putting hooks inside conditionals or loops
Mixing global state libraries when plain React hooks are preferred

faq.question: What React features does this skill cover?
faq.answer: React 19 functional components, useState, useMemo, useCallback, useRef, and useEffect with proper dependency arrays.

faq.question: What TypeScript rules are enforced?
faq.answer: No `any` types, strict null checks, explicit interfaces for all data structures, and type inference when obvious.

faq.question: How does this integrate with Claude Code?
faq.answer: The skill provides documented patterns that Claude Code follows when generating or reviewing React 19 and TypeScript code.

faq.question: Is my data safe with this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. This skill only contains reference documentation. It does not access files, networks, or execute any code.

faq.question: Why is there a 200-line component limit?
faq.answer: Smaller components are easier to test, maintain, and debug. Larger components should be split into focused sub-components.

faq.question: How does this compare to eslint?
faq.answer: This skill documents architectural patterns and conventions. It complements linters by covering design and organization rules.
